title: TPN & Central Line Complications
subtitle: 59 y/o female on parenteral nutrition with a rising glucose and fever
nursesNote: 0600 - 59 y/o female, post-op day 6 after bowel resection with a prolonged ileus, receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN) through a tunneled central venous catheter for the past 4 days. Overnight she developed a temperature of 101.6F with shaking chills, and the 0600 fingerstick glucose is 268 mg/dL (her values had been running 120-150). The central line insertion site is reddened with a small amount of purulent drainage. The current TPN bag has been hanging for 26 hours. She reports feeling 'awful' but denies chest pain or shortness of breath.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) findings that MOST suggest a central line-associated infection.

opts.text: Temperature 101.6F with shaking chills
opts.rat: New fever with rigors in a client with a central line is a classic sign of a catheter-related bloodstream infection.

opts.text: Redness and purulent drainage at the central line insertion site
opts.rat: Local erythema with purulent drainage points directly to infection at the catheter site.

opts.text: WBC elevated at 17.4 K/uL
opts.rat: Leukocytosis supports an active infectious process.

opts.text: New tachycardia (118) with a falling blood pressure
opts.rat: Rising heart rate with a dropping blood pressure suggests a systemic inflammatory response and possible early sepsis.

opts.text: Albumin of 2.6 g/dL
opts.rat: Low albumin reflects her underlying nutritional/surgical status, not the acute infection.

opts.text: Potassium of 3.4 mEq/L
opts.rat: A mildly low potassium is a TPN electrolyte issue to correct, not a sign of catheter infection.

opts.text: She denies chest pain or shortness of breath
opts.rat: The absence of these symptoms is reassuring against other complications; it is not a cue for infection.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret this situation.

opts.text: TPN's high glucose concentration makes the central line a high-risk site for infection
opts.rat: The dextrose-rich solution is an excellent medium for microbial growth, which is why strict sterile technique and site care are essential with TPN.

opts.text: The rising glucose may reflect both the TPN dextrose load and the stress of infection
opts.rat: Hyperglycemia is a known TPN complication and is worsened by the counter-regulatory hormones released during infection/sepsis.

opts.text: A TPN bag hanging 26 hours exceeds the recommended hang time and increases infection risk
opts.rat: TPN bags are generally changed within 24 hours; prolonged hang time increases the risk of microbial growth.

opts.text: The TPN should simply be stopped abruptly and no further glucose monitoring is needed
opts.rat: Abruptly stopping TPN risks rebound hypoglycemia, and glucose must continue to be monitored closely.

opts.text: Fever in a client with a central line is expected and requires no investigation
opts.rat: New fever with a central line always warrants evaluation for a catheter-related bloodstream infection.

opts.text: TPN can safely be interrupted to infuse other medications through the same lumen
opts.rat: The TPN lumen is dedicated; mixing other infusions increases contamination and incompatibility risk.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the explanations from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Central line-associated bloodstream infection from the TPN catheter
opts.rat: Fever with rigors, purulent site drainage, leukocytosis, and hemodynamic change in a client with a TPN central line make catheter infection most likely.

opts.text: TPN-related hyperglycemia contributing to the picture
opts.rat: Hyperglycemia is a real and expected TPN complication here, compounding infection risk, though it does not explain the fever and purulent drainage.

opts.text: Surgical site or intra-abdominal infection from the recent bowel resection
opts.rat: A plausible alternative source that must be evaluated, but the purulent central line site points more directly to the catheter.

opts.text: Postoperative atelectasis or pneumonia
opts.rat: Less likely given normal oxygen saturation and no respiratory complaints, though fever workup should remain broad.

opts.text: Air embolism from the central line
opts.rat: Least likely: air embolism presents suddenly with respiratory distress and chest pain, which she denies.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate interventions.

opts.text: Notify the provider promptly and anticipate blood cultures (including from the line) before antibiotics
opts.rat: Prompt notification and obtaining cultures before starting antibiotics identify the organism and guide therapy.

opts.text: Continue close glucose monitoring and administer insulin coverage as ordered
opts.rat: TPN-related hyperglycemia worsened by infection requires frequent monitoring and ordered insulin coverage.

opts.text: Replace the TPN bag and tubing per policy using strict sterile technique
opts.rat: The bag has exceeded its hang time; changing bag and tubing with sterile technique removes a potential contamination source.

opts.text: Assess and care for the insertion site, and monitor vital signs closely for sepsis
opts.rat: Site assessment/care and close hemodynamic monitoring detect worsening infection and progression toward sepsis.

opts.text: Abruptly discontinue the TPN and hang nothing in its place
opts.rat: Stopping TPN abruptly can cause rebound hypoglycemia; a dextrose-containing solution is typically ordered if TPN must be interrupted.

opts.text: Use the TPN lumen to give the new IV antibiotic
opts.rat: The TPN lumen is dedicated; using it for other infusions raises contamination and incompatibility risks.

opts.text: Increase the TPN infusion rate to improve her nutrition and help her fight the infection
opts.rat: Increasing the rate would worsen the hyperglycemia and does not treat the infection.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Assess the client fully-vital signs, insertion site, and level of consciousness
opts.rat: FIRST-a focused assessment establishes how sick she is and confirms the suspected source.

opts.text: Notify the provider of the fever, purulent site, and hemodynamic changes
opts.rat: SECOND-early notification mobilizes cultures, antibiotics, and line-management decisions.

opts.text: Obtain blood cultures as ordered before antibiotics are started
opts.rat: THIRD-cultures drawn before antibiotics preserve the ability to identify the organism.

opts.text: Administer ordered antibiotics and insulin coverage
opts.rat: FOURTH-treatment proceeds once cultures are obtained and orders are in place.

opts.text: Change the TPN bag and tubing with sterile technique and document the event
opts.rat: LAST of these steps-removing the expired bag and documenting completes the immediate response.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Over the next 24 hours, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: Temperature returns to 98.8F and the chills resolve after antibiotics
opts.rat: Defervescence and resolution of rigors suggest the infection is responding to treatment.

opts.text: Glucose stabilizes at 128-145 mg/dL with the ordered insulin coverage
opts.rat: Glucose returning toward her usual range shows the TPN-related hyperglycemia is being controlled.

opts.text: The insertion site is clean and dry with no drainage after line replacement
opts.rat: A clean, dry site after removing the infected catheter indicates the local source has been addressed.

opts.text: Blood pressure falls to 82/44 with a lactate of 4.2 mmol/L
opts.rat: Hypotension with a rising lactate signals progression to septic shock requiring immediate escalation.

opts.text: Glucose drops to 48 mg/dL after the TPN is stopped without a dextrose replacement
opts.rat: This is rebound hypoglycemia from abrupt TPN discontinuation-a preventable complication needing immediate treatment.

opts.text: Blood cultures grow an organism and the client develops new confusion
opts.rat: Confirmed bacteremia with new altered mental status indicates worsening systemic infection.

opts.text: Potassium remains 3.4 mEq/L with no change after the TPN formula review
opts.rat: A persistently borderline potassium shows the electrolyte issue has not yet been corrected in the TPN formulation.

opts.text: Albumin remains 2.6 g/dL
opts.rat: Albumin changes slowly and would not be expected to shift meaningfully in 24 hours; it is neither better nor worse.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
